post.author: Story Makers
post.caption: Story Makers · FollowStory Makers · Original audioAdrian had only wanted ten quiet minutes with his daughter.The autumn park was calm, almost too calm. Leaves covered the wooden bench. The air smelled cold and dry. Rina sat close beside him in her dark sunglasses, her white cane resting across her knees. She looked small, fragile, innocent.Then suddenly, a dirty little boy grabbed Adrian’s sleeve.His fingers were shaking. His clothes were torn. His old backpack hung open. He looked terrified — but he would not let go.Adrian turned sharply.“What did you just say?”The boy leaned closer, breathing hard.“Your daughter isn’t blind.”For a second, Adrian just stared at him.Rina stayed still.Too still.A leaf drifted down in front of her face.And then it happened.Her head turned.Her eyes followed it behind the dark glasses.The cane slipped from her lap — and her hand shot out fast enough to catch it before it fell.Adrian felt the blood drain from his face.He looked at the boy again.“What?”The boy swallowed.“I saw her look.”Adrian’s heartbeat turned violent.He looked at Rina. Then over his shoulder.Far down the path, a woman was jogging toward them through the blurred trees.His wife.The boy’s voice dropped to a whisper.“I sleep near your house.”Adrian went still.“What did you see?”The boy pointed weakly toward the jogging woman.His lips trembled.“It’s your wife… she puts something in her food.”Adrian’s hand loosened from shock.Beside him, Rina slowly turned her face toward the path.Then, in a tiny broken whisper, she said:“Daddy… please don’t tell her I can see today.”Adrian stopped breathing.The woman in the distance suddenly slowed down.Continuation in the first comment. article.title: 🎬PART 2։ What the Girl Was Hiding
article.content:
Adrian dropped to his knees in front of Rina.

His voice came out broken.

“What did you say?”

Rina’s little mouth trembled.

Tears slid from beneath her sunglasses.

“She told me not to tell you.”

The dirty boy stepped back, looking like he already regretted speaking. But it was too late now.

Adrian’s eyes locked on his daughter.

“Who told you that?”

Rina clutched the cane with both hands.

“Celia.”

Not “Mom.”

Not even “Mama.”

Just Celia.

A cold fear ripped through Adrian’s chest.

The woman on the path was closer now. Still jogging. Still smiling. Still pretending everything was normal.

Adrian pulled the sunglasses off Rina’s face.

Her eyes were wide open.

Clear.

Focused.

Terrified.

“How long?” he whispered.

Rina started crying.

“I could always see a little… but she said if I told you, you’d go away. She said the medicine would help me. But it made me sleepy… and sick…”

Adrian felt sick himself.

The boy spoke again, his voice shaking.

“I saw her throw the real food away. I saw her change the bottles.”

Adrian turned to him in horror.

“Why didn’t you tell anyone?”

The boy’s eyes filled with tears.

“Because nobody listens to boys like me.”

The footsteps stopped behind them.

A shadow fell across the bench.

Celia’s voice came soft and sweet.

“Adrian… what’s going on?”

Adrian stood up slowly, holding Rina behind him now.

For the first time, he looked at his wife like she was a stranger.

Rina buried her face against his back and whispered:

“She knows that boy saw her.”

Adrian lifted his eyes to Celia.

And then he noticed it.

In her jogging pocket—

a small amber bottle.

The same one Rina had just described.

Celia smiled.

But this time, Adrian saw something behind the smile that he had never let himself see before.

And the boy took one frightened step backward.

Because Celia wasn’t looking at Adrian anymore.

She was looking at him.
